item.title: WSW Artist's Book Residency Grant
item.description:
Organisation: Women's Studio Workshop (WSW)

Location: Rosendale, NY, USA; in-person residency.

Award: $350/week stipend; up to $1,000 for materials; up to $250 for travel within the Continental US; free onsite housing; 24/7 studio access. No application fee.

Duration: 8-10 weeks; residency runs September through June, one year after application.

Deadline: 15 Nov 2026

Women's Studio Workshop (WSW) offers an eight- to ten-week residency for artists to produce a limited-edition artist's book. Working intensively in WSW's studios in Rosendale, NY, selected artists print and bind their own books; edition size must be between 50 and 100 copies. AMOUNT: $350/week stipend; up to $1,000 for materials; up to $250 for travel within the Continental US; free onsite housing; 24/7 studio access. WSW provides technical advice, training on new equipment, techniques, and materials, and production assistance. SALES CONTRACT: 20% of the edition goes to the artist; 20% (at least nine copies) stays with WSW for archival and institutional sales; 60% goes to general distribution and sale. If the artist sells through WSW, WSW takes a 30% commission plus 20% for marketing; the artist receives a 50% commission, paid annually around 30 June. WSW holds the right to sell distribution copies at a 20% discount to standing-order and repository institutions. WSW's books are held in over 450 collections worldwide, including the Library of Congress, Yale University, and Vassar College. An outside jury of artists and curators selects recipients. INDIGENOUS ARTISTS: applicants may check the appropriate box on the Submittable form to be considered for the BIMA Artist's Book Residency Grant for Indigenous Artists. APPLICATION MATERIALS (upload via Submittable): current resume; brief project description including media required (200-word maximum); materials budget form (Google Sheets, Excel, or PDF); up to ten images of recent work; image script (title, medium, dimensions, date). MAILED MATERIALS (to WSW at the address below): a physical book mock-up demonstrating how the book will be bound or function, actual size, with at least 1-2 fully realised spreads; and the shipping form (download and open the PDF locally). Mail to: Artist's Book Application, Women's Studio Workshop, PO Box 489, Rosendale, NY 12472. Applications open 1 August 2026; deadline 15 November 2026, 11:59pm EST; notification March 2027; residency runs September through June, one year after application. Apply via Submittable at https://wsworkshop.org/residencies/artists-book-residency-grant/.

Open call details

item.title: Horizon Europe Cluster 2: Culture, Creativity and Inclusive Society (HORIZON-CL2-2026-01-HERITAGE) — incl. AI and Creative Content topic
item.description:
Organisation: European Research Executive Agency (REA) / European Commission, Horizon Europe

Location: European Union and associated countries. Consortium-based; typically requires partners from multiple EU/Horizon member states.

Award: Indicative total budget €85.5 million across 7 topics (2026 call). Individual project amounts vary by topic. Up to 100% funding rate for non-profit research organizations. No application fee.

Duration: Typically 3-4 years; varies by topic.

Deadline: 23 Sep 2026

Horizon Europe Cluster 2 funds research on culture, creativity, and inclusive society. The 2026 call (HORIZON-CL2-2026-01-HERITAGE, launched May 12, 2026) covers 7 topics with an indicative budget of €85.5 million. Topic HERITAGE-04 is explicitly titled 'Towards a fair and transparent market for cultural and creative content in the era of generative AI,' covering: impacts of generative AI on creative industries employment; upskilling needs for creative workers; policy and regulatory frameworks; fair remuneration and transparency. Highly relevant for research into AI and cultural/creative industries, artists' rights in the AI era, and sociotechnical studies of AI and culture. ELIGIBILITY: consortium-based applications (minimum 3 organisations from 3 different EU/associated countries are typical for Horizon RIA grants; check call conditions on the Funding & Tenders portal for specific requirements). Deadline: 23 September 2026. Apply via the EU Funding & Tenders portal (ec.europa.eu/info/funding-tenders).

Open call details

item.title: Freelands Foundation: Learning Through Making Fund
item.description:
Organisation: Freelands Foundation

Location: UK (England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, Wales); activity must occur within the United Kingdom.

Award: Up to £25,000. No application fee.

Duration: Projects of any length, taking place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. Funding provided no earlier than December 2026.

Deadline: 11 Sep 2026

The Learning Through Making fund supports UK-based visual art organisations to bring opportunities for making and material exploration into their art education work. Freelands Foundation is looking for exploratory projects that embrace openness, collaboration, and iteration -- enabling organisations to work with materials in an expansive way, unpicking preconceptions about making and material literacy without predefined outcomes. ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES: providing materials, transport, or access to expertise and facilities; action-research trials such as a new series of workshops or events developing material literacy; adding a making-focused element to existing activity. Projects may range from a few days to several months, but must take place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. AMOUNT: up to £25,000; funding provided no earlier than December 2026. ELIGIBILITY: UK-based visual art organisations run with a charitable purpose and for public benefit; eligible types include charities registered in England and Wales, Scotland, or Northern Ireland; exempt charities; galleries, museums, or organisations within a Local Authority or larger charitable body (e.g. universities); and Community Interest Companies or Community Benefit Societies meeting specific criteria (charitable purposes, asset lock, no dividend distribution, ability to segregate restricted funds). Organisations with a prior Freelands Foundation grant relationship are eligible; you may not apply for activity already funded by Freelands Foundation. Individuals are not eligible. HOW TO APPLY: download the Application Form, Application Guidance, and optional Access Form from freelandsfoundation.co.uk; email completed form to grants@freelandsfoundation.co.uk with subject line 'LTM Fund - Application'; one additional page of images or diagrams may be included. APPLICATION SUPPORT SESSIONS (online): 9 July 2026, 10:30am-12pm; 12 August 2026, 10:30am-12pm. KEY DATES: applications open 17 June 2026; close 11 September 2026 at 12pm; outcomes shared 23 November 2026.

Open call details

item.title: AI Research Fellow / Senior AI Policy Researcher (Utah Office of AI Policy)
item.description:
Organisation: Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy

Location: Salt Lake City, Utah, United States. Hybrid telework may be available but regular in-person work is required, and in-state work rules require all work to be performed while physically within Utah. No visa sponsorship.

Award: Salary: USD 33.13 to 54.01 hourly wage. Full-time, time-limited position. Job classification Research Consultant III (#16048); may underfill as Research Consultant II (#16058) with a reduced salary range.

Duration: Full-time, time-limited (Schedule Code TL).

Deadline: Closed 24 Jun 2026

The Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy, is seeking an AI Research Fellow to help drive technical, scientific, and policy research for Utah's AI Learning Lab. This is an applied research and policy-translation role whose work should directly inform regulatory decisions, legislative recommendations, regulatory mitigation agreements, and stakeholder strategy. The role is technical and relies heavily on statistical, machine learning, computer science, and empirical research skills, while also requiring strong judgment, clear writing, and the ability to operate in a small multidisciplinary team alongside colleagues with policy, business, regulatory, and legal expertise. The Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy was created in May 2024 to systematically develop sensible AI policy across major impact areas such as healthcare, education, finance, transportation, and consumer technology. Central to the Office is the AI Learning Lab, where industry experts, academics, regulators, and policymakers work toward appropriate regulatory responses to emerging AI applications; the Office also has authority to create regulatory mitigation agreements that let responsible AI businesses test products in Utah under structured conditions while the state gathers evidence. KEY RESPONSIBILITIES: independently scope and execute research projects on emerging AI policy questions (defining the question, identifying technical and empirical evidence, interviewing experts, evaluating tradeoffs, and producing written recommendations); research the technical aspects, capabilities, limitations, and societal impacts of AI technologies; evaluate evidence and forecasts on the impact of human-AI interactions on health, economic activity, education, consumer welfare, and public institutions; draft executive policy memos, legislative briefings, technical explainers, risk assessments, and regulatory mitigation analyses; interface with business leaders, technologists, researchers, regulators, legislators, and lawyers; and advise on the development of regulatory and legal standards. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: deep technical knowledge of statistical methods, machine learning, and AI systems; strong ability to translate technical and empirical evidence into practical policy options for nontechnical decision-makers; familiarity with human-subject research, qualitative and quantitative methods, medical or health sciences, social sciences, human-computer interaction, or related empirical fields; excellent problem-solving, reliability, and independent judgment; and strong technical communication across policy, legal, regulatory, business, and technical audiences. PREFERRED: experience implementing, evaluating, or deploying AI systems (business, healthcare, government, or other applied settings); expertise in public policy, law, regulatory design, or government decision-making; ability to evaluate technical claims, research methods, and evidence quality; specific healthcare experience; and experience supporting AI deployment in regulated environments. A criminal history check is required. Recruiter: Tehra Gorski, tgorski@utah.gov, 801-530-6030. Application deadline 24 June 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AY-ROOP / Le Milieu: Reuse - (Re)making for Circus Residency 2026 (Rennes Métropole, France)
item.description:
Organisation: AY-ROOP

Location: Le Milieu, Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole, France. Applicants must legally reside in a Creative Europe country other than France and the French overseas territories (DROM-COM).

Award: Funded through Culture Moves Europe: EUR 900 mobility allowance per person plus flat-rate travel coverage; supplements for green mobility, visa fees, dependent children under 18, OCT/OR residents, and disability/accessibility needs (including possible full coverage of a sign-language interpreter). On-site single-room accommodation, lunches at Le Milieu canteen (Tuesday to Friday), shared kitchen, and technical support provided. No application fee.

Duration: 21-day residency, 28 November to 18 December 2026.

Deadline: 03 Jul 2026

AY-ROOP is launching an open call to select three circus artists and/or circus set designers/builders for a 21-day Culture Moves Europe research residency at Le Milieu (Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole) from 28 November to 18 December 2026. The residency is a research laboratory on reuse in contemporary circus: how everyday, locally available materials (paper/cardboard, plastics, lightweight metal, textiles, reclaimed wood, bicycle parts, etc.) can be used to create scenographic elements, sets, props and small apparatuses for juggling, acrobatics and balancing. The core principle is that objects are recreated locally from resources available in each territory rather than transported. AY-ROOP provides an equipped construction workshop, a rehearsal stage and circus tent, a guide to local reuse-sector contacts, and a full residency environment (accommodation, shared kitchen, working facilities); two mentors (a reuse specialist and a performing-arts professional) support the artists. PROFILES: circus artists interested in construction/scenography/DIY making, and builders/set designers working in circus; individual or group applications (max 3 people per group); emerging or established, any background. Accessible to artists with disabilities and accessibility needs. Working language English. No aerial apparatus or rigging: all work is ground-based. ELIGIBILITY: aged 18+ (no upper limit); legally resident in a Creative Europe country other than France and DROM-COM; available for the full residency. APPLY with a single PDF (1-page bio/CV, 5-page portfolio or video links, 1-page statement of intent, proof of residence). DEADLINE: 3 July 2026. Results announced 13 July 2026. Check the linked call PDF for full details.

Open call details
